section.title: Epilogue
section.sectionRationale: This closing unit functions as the epilogue not only of Revelation but, canonically, of the entire Christian Bible. The angel authenticates the whole vision as faithful and true, John's premature worship of the angel is corrected with 'Worship God,' Jesus speaks in the first person three times promising to come soon, a solemn warning is issued against tampering with the prophecy, and the book closes with the church's ancient prayer, 'Come, Lord Jesus,' and a benediction of grace. It is bounded by the angel's opening authentication in verse 6 and the grace-benediction that ends the whole book in verse 21.
section.thesis: The Lord who has shown these true and faithful words is coming soon; His people are to keep, not alter, this prophecy, to wash their robes, and to answer His promise with the church's ancient prayer, 'Come, Lord Jesus,' resting in His grace until He does.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the angel's authentication of the prophecy and the blessing on those who keep it; John's misdirected worship of the angel and the correction, 'Worship God'; Jesus' declaration, 'I am coming soon,' with His reward and the final either/or of blessing or exclusion; His self-testimony as the Root and Offspring of David, the bright Morning Star, and the Spirit and the bride's invitation to come; and the closing warning against adding to or taking from the prophecy, the prayer 'Come, Lord Jesus,' and the benediction of grace.

units.label: These words are faithful and true

verses.text: Then the angel said to me, “These words are faithful and true. The Lord, the God of the spirits of the prophets, has sent His angel to show His servants what must {{tachy|soon}} take place.”
“Behold, I am coming {{tachy|soon}}. Blessed is the one who keeps the words of prophecy in this book.”
And I am John, who heard and saw these things. And when I had heard and seen them, I fell down to worship at the feet of the angel who had shown me these things.
But he said to me, “Do not do that! I am a fellow servant with you and your brothers the prophets, and with those who keep the words of this book. Worship God!”
Then he told me, “Do not seal up the words of prophecy in this book, because the time is near.
Let the unrighteous continue to be unrighteous, and the vile continue to be vile; let the righteous continue to practice righteousness, and the holy continue to be holy.”

units.label: I am coming soon, with My reward

verses.text: “Behold, I am coming {{tachy|soon}}, and My reward is with Me, to give to each one according to what he has done.
I am the Alpha and the Omega, the First and the Last, the Beginning and the End.”
Blessed are those who wash their robes, so that they may have the right to the tree of life and may enter the city by its gates.
But outside are the dogs, the sorcerers, the sexually immoral, the murderers, the idolaters, and everyone who loves and practices falsehood.

units.label: The Root and Offspring of David, the Morning Star
units.note: Jesus' own testimony, and the invitation to come

verses.text: “I, Jesus, have sent My angel to give you this testimony for the churches. I am the Root and the Offspring of David, the bright {{aster_proinos|Morning Star}}.”
The Spirit and the bride say, “{{erchou|Come}}!” Let the one who hears say, “{{erchou|Come}}!” And let the one who is thirsty come, and the one who desires the water of life drink freely.

units.label: The warning, the prayer, and the benediction
units.note: do not add or take away; Come, Lord Jesus

verses.text: I testify to everyone who hears the words of prophecy in this book: If anyone adds to them, God will add to him the plagues described in this book.
And if anyone takes away from the words of this book of prophecy, God will take away his share in the tree of life and the holy city, which are described in this book.
He who testifies to these things says, “Yes, I am {{tachy|coming soon}}.” Amen. {{erchou|Come}}, Lord Jesus!
The {{charis|grace}} of the Lord Jesus be with all the saints. Amen.

tachy.gloss: Quickly, soon, with speed. Used three times in this chapter of Christ's coming, it is debated whether it names calendar nearness or the suddenness and certainty of the event once it begins; either sense presses believers toward constant, watchful readiness rather than complacency.

aster_proinos.gloss: The Morning Star, the bright star that heralds the dawn. Jesus takes this title for Himself, fulfilling the star that would rise out of Jacob, Numbers 24:17, and named again as a sign of certain hope in 2 Peter 1:19.

erchou.gloss: Come, an imperative from erchomai, to come or go. It is the Spirit and the bride's invitation, the hearer's echo of it, and finally the church's own prayer addressed to the returning Christ, 'Come, Lord Jesus,' in verse 20.

charis.gloss: Grace, unmerited favor. It is the very last word given for the whole book of Revelation, and by canonical placement, the closing note of the entire Christian Bible, a fitting seal on a book so full of warning and wonder.

basic.context: The angel closes the vision by insisting every word of it is faithful and true. John is so overwhelmed he falls down to worship the angel, but is corrected at once, 'Worship God.' Jesus then speaks directly three times, promising 'I am coming soon,' before the book ends with a warning not to tamper with its words and the church's prayer, 'Come, Lord Jesus.'
basic.hermeneutics: Readers debate what 'soon' means after two thousand years, some reading it as imminent readiness for every generation, others as prophetic language about the suddenness of the event rather than its calendar timing. Either way, the text calls for watchful, faithful living now, not date-setting.
basic.originalLanguages: Christ says He is coming tachy, soon or swiftly, three times. He calls Himself the Morning Star, aster ho proinos, the herald of the dawn. The Spirit and the bride cry erchou, Come, and the whole book ends on charis, grace, its very last word.
basic.typology: The tree of life and the holy city, once barred from fallen humanity, are now freely promised to those who wash their robes. The warning against adding to or removing from this book echoes Moses' warning about the Law, and the prayer 'Come, Lord Jesus' echoes the ancient Aramaic cry Maranatha.
basic.christ: Jesus speaks in the first person, 'I, Jesus,' naming Himself Root and Offspring of David and the bright Morning Star, both fully divine and truly human, David's Lord and David's descendant, promising to come soon and to reward each according to what has been done.
basic.god: God is the God of the spirits of the prophets who sent His angel, whose words are faithful and true, who warns against tampering with them, and whose grace is the very last word of the whole Bible.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 4:2 and 12:32 warn against adding to or taking from God's word. Numbers 24:17 and Isaiah 11:1, 10 promise the Star and Root of David. 1 Corinthians 16:22 preserves the same prayer in Aramaic, Maranatha, Come, our Lord.
basic.oneStory: The Bible that began with humanity barred from the tree of life by a flaming sword ends with an open invitation, 'Come,' and the tree of life freely promised to those who wash their robes, sealed by the grace of the Lord Jesus and the church's ancient prayer for His return.

context.literary: Functions as the epilogue of the whole book, mirroring and answering its prologue in 1:1-3, faithful and true words, a blessing on those who keep them, and the nearness of what must take place. It moves from the angel's authentication, through John's own error and its correction, to Christ's direct first-person speech, and closes with a warning, a prayer, and a benediction, the classic shape of an ancient letter's closing greetings.
context.historical: Written to churches facing real pressure to compromise or apostatize, this epilogue presses the urgency of Christ's promised return and guards the integrity of the prophetic word at a time when false teachers and false prophecies were already circulating, Revelation 2:2, 20.
context.canonical: As the final verses of the last book of the New Testament, this epilogue closes not only Revelation but, by its canonical position, the whole Christian Bible; its warning against adding or removing words has long been read as guarding the completed shape of Scripture itself, and its closing prayer, 'Come, Lord Jesus,' has been the church's prayer ever since.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To authenticate the entire vision as trustworthy revelation from God, to press the urgency of Christ's promised return on His hearers, to protect the integrity of the prophetic word from addition or subtraction, and to close the whole book in worship, warning, invitation, and grace.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The epilogue both describes what is certainly coming, Christ's return and reward, and prescribes a present response: worship God alone, keep these words rather than altering them, wash your robes, and pray for Christ's coming rather than growing complacent or afraid.

debates.question: In what sense is Jesus 'coming soon,' Revelation 22:7, 12, 20, given that two thousand years have passed?

views.label: 'Soon' as genuine nearness, calling every generation to live in readiness
views.case: From the perspective of the church's mission, Christ's return could come at any moment for any generation, and 'soon' names a posture of constant readiness rather than a fixed date; every believer since John has rightly lived expecting Him imminently.

views.label: 'Soon' as prophetic idiom describing certainty and suddenness, not calendar proximity
views.case: In prophetic speech, words for speed often describe how swiftly and unstoppably an event unfolds once begun, not its nearness on a calendar; 2 Peter 3:8 reminds readers that with the Lord a day is like a thousand years, so two thousand years poses no difficulty, when Christ comes it will be sudden and certain.

debates.bearing: Both readings call believers to the same posture: alert, watchful, holy living now, refusing either to set dates or to grow complacent, echoing Jesus' own refusal to give a timetable in Acts 1:7.

study.originalLanguages: Christ declares Himself erchomai tachy, coming soon, three times across this closing chapter, a word debated between calendar nearness and prophetic suddenness. He names Himself aster ho proinos, the Morning Star, the herald of dawn who fulfills the star promised to rise out of Jacob. The Spirit and the bride, and every hearer after them, cry erchou, Come, an invitation that becomes, in the very last lines of the book, the church's own prayer addressed to Christ Himself. And the last word given to the whole of Revelation, and by its place in the canon to the whole Bible, is charis, grace.

typology.type: The tree of life and the holy city, barred from fallen humanity, Genesis 3:22-24
typology.fulfillment: Freely promised here to those who wash their robes, Revelation 22:14, the final resolution of Eden's exile.

typology.type: The command not to add to or take from God's word, Deuteronomy 4:2, 12:32
typology.fulfillment: Reapplied to this book, and by canonical extension understood to guard the completed Scriptures as a whole.

typology.type: The Star out of Jacob and the Root of Jesse, Numbers 24:17, Isaiah 11:1, 10
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Jesus, both David's ancestor as God and David's descendant as man, the Root and Offspring of David, the bright Morning Star.
Echoed directly in the closing 'Come, Lord Jesus,' the church's persistent longing for Christ's return preserved across languages and centuries.

study.christ: Jesus speaks here in His own first-person voice, 'I, Jesus,' three times declaring 'I am coming soon' and naming Himself the Alpha and the Omega, the Root and the Offspring of David, and the bright Morning Star, titles that hold together His full deity and His true, human descent from David. He promises reward according to deeds done, while the invitation to the thirsty to drink of the water of life freely shows that grace, not merit, remains the ground on which anyone enters the city. His threefold promise to come soon frames the whole epilogue and answers the church's oldest prayer, 'Come, Lord Jesus.'
study.god: God is the God of the spirits of the prophets, who sent His angel to show His servants what must soon take place; His words are faithful and true, and He warns solemnly against anyone adding to or subtracting from them. He alone is to be worshiped, not any angel or messenger, however glorious, and His grace, given through the Lord Jesus, is fittingly the very last word of the whole Bible.

crossReferences.note: The command not to add to or take away from God's word, echoed directly in the warning of Revelation 22:18-19.
The Star out of Jacob and the Root of Jesse, fulfilled in Christ's self-titles here, the Root and Offspring of David, the Morning Star.
Preserves the same prayer in its Aramaic form, Maranatha, our Lord, come, echoed in the Greek 'Come, Lord Jesus' of Revelation 22:20.
Jesus' own refusal to give His disciples a timetable for His return, bearing on how 'coming soon' should be read here.

study.oneStory: The Bible that began with humanity barred from the tree of life by a flaming sword, Genesis 3:24, ends with an open door: 'Blessed are those who wash their robes, so that they may have the right to the tree of life and may enter the city by its gates.' The very last word of Scripture is not a threat but an invitation, 'Come,' and a prayer gathering up every longing since Eden, 'Come, Lord Jesus,' sealed with grace for all the saints.

sermon.title: Even So, Come, Lord Jesus!
sermon.why: Carson's sermon takes its title directly from this epilogue's closing prayer in 22:20 and preaches through Revelation 21:1-22:6, culminating in the church's response to Christ's promise, I am coming soon, with the ancient cry, Even so, come, Lord Jesus.

quiz.q: Why does the angel refuse John's worship in verses 8-9?

options.text: Because only God is to be worshiped; the angel is a fellow servant, not an object of worship.
options.note: The angel says plainly, 'Worship God!'

options.text: Because John had not properly prepared himself to worship.
options.note: The issue is who is worshiped, not John's readiness or preparation.

options.text: Because angels can never be honored in any way.
options.note: The angel is honored as a fellow servant; the objection is specifically to worship, which belongs to God alone.

options.text: Because the vision itself was false and unworthy of a response.
options.note: The angel has just called the words faithful and true; the correction concerns who receives worship, not the vision's truth.

quiz.q: What does Jesus' repeated statement, 'I am coming soon,' most likely convey?

options.text: The certainty and appropriate urgency of Christ's return, whether read as near in time or sudden once it begins.
options.note: Both major readings of 'soon' agree on certainty and the call to readiness.

options.text: A precise date that can be calculated from the surrounding text.
options.note: Revelation gives no calculable date; Acts 1:7 records Jesus refusing to disclose times or seasons.

options.text: That Christ had already returned by the time John wrote this.
options.note: The promise is future and repeated three times as still awaited.

options.text: That the promise of Christ's return no longer applies today.
options.note: The promise and the church's prayer for it remain live and repeated.

quiz.q: What is the warning in verses 18-19 about adding to or taking from the prophecy?

options.text: It echoes Deuteronomy's warning against tampering with God's word and guards the integrity of this book's message.
options.note: Deuteronomy 4:2 and 12:32 use nearly identical language.

options.text: It forbids anyone from ever reading the book of Revelation aloud.
options.note: The warning concerns altering the words, not reading or teaching them.

options.text: It applies only to scribes copying manuscripts in the ancient world.
options.note: While it addressed textual integrity then, its principle of guarding God's word carries ongoing weight.

options.text: It has no connection to any other part of Scripture.
options.note: It directly echoes the Deuteronomy warnings named above.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean calling Himself the Root and Offspring of David, the Morning Star, in verse 16?

options.text: That He is both David's divine source and his human descendant, and the star that fulfills ancient hope and heralds the dawn.
options.note: Root names His deity as David's source; Offspring names His true humanity as David's descendant.

options.text: That He was merely one more king in David's line, no different from the others.
options.note: The title Root sets Him above David as well as descended from him, unlike any other king in the line.

options.text: That He replaces David's line entirely with a new, unrelated dynasty.
options.note: He is explicitly David's own offspring, not a replacement for the line.

options.text: That the title has no connection to Old Testament promises.
options.note: It directly fulfills Numbers 24:17 and Isaiah 11:1, 10.

quiz.q: How does the church answer Christ's promise to come soon, in verses 17 and 20?

options.text: With the invitation and prayer, 'Come,' echoed by the Spirit, the bride, and every hearer, and finally addressed to Christ Himself.
options.note: This is the shape of verses 17 and 20, ending in 'Come, Lord Jesus.'

options.text: With silence, since no response to Christ's promise is recorded.
options.note: The text records an explicit, repeated response, 'Come.'

options.text: With a request that Christ delay His coming as long as possible.
options.note: The prayer asks for His coming, not for delay.

options.text: With a demand for proof before believing the promise.
options.note: The response is invitation and prayer, not demand or doubt.

questions: John, overwhelmed by the vision, tried to worship the angel rather than God. Where in your own life might awe or gratitude toward a person, gift, or experience be pulling your worship away from God alone?
Jesus says, 'Behold, I am coming soon,' three times in this chapter. Does that promise feel near and real to you, or distant? What would change if you lived today as though it were true?

The warning against adding to or taking from God's word is a serious one. Are there ways, even subtle ones, that you're tempted to add your own rules or subtract Christ's hard sayings from what Scripture actually teaches?
'Let the one who is thirsty come, and the one who desires the water of life drink freely.' What is keeping you from coming freely to Christ today, rather than trying to earn or deserve His welcome?
The whole Bible ends with the prayer, 'Come, Lord Jesus,' and the blessing, 'grace to all the saints.' How might praying that prayer regularly change the way you face this season of waiting?

translationNotes.note: 'Coming soon' translates tachy, a word that in prophetic idiom can describe the suddenness and certainty of an event as much as its calendar nearness; readers differ on which sense is primary here, though both call for present readiness.

'Alpha and the Omega, the First and the Last, the Beginning and the End' stacks three near-synonymous titles of eternity together, underscoring by repetition Christ's total sovereignty over history rather than adding new information with each phrase.

The warning against adding to or taking away from 'the words of this book' closely echoes Deuteronomy 4:2 and 12:32's warnings about the Law, applied here to this specific prophetic book, and traditionally understood as bearing on the integrity of the whole of Scripture.

'Come, Lord Jesus' renders in Greek the same prayer 1 Corinthians 16:22 preserves in its original Aramaic, Maranatha, meaning 'our Lord, come' or 'our Lord has come,' one of the earliest recorded prayers of the church.

meta.notes: BSB text supplied and pre-verified this session; no em or en dashes were present in the supplied text and none were introduced. Poetic line breaks were already flattened into flowing prose as supplied. Straight quotation marks were converted to curly typographic marks, and the continuous divine speech running from verse 10 into verse 11, and from verse 12 into verse 13, was punctuated as a single closed quotation to match standard BSB rendering; no wording was altered.
